atomic.name: eps
atomic.math: Machine epsilon, the smallest positive floating point number greater than zero
atomic.desc: Represents the smallest positive number that can be added to 1 to yield a result different from 1.

atomic.name: maxf
atomic.math: The maximum positive representable floating-point number
atomic.desc: Represents the maximum value of a floating-point number.

atomic.name: minf
atomic.math: The minimum positive representable floating-point number
atomic.desc: Represents the minimum positive value of a floating-point number.

atomic.name: iinf
atomic.desc: Represents positive infinity, used in floating-point calculations.

atomic.name: ninf
atomic.desc: Represents negative infinity, used in floating-point calculations.

atomic.name: nan
atomic.desc: Represents an undefined or unrepresentable value resulting from certain mathematical operations.

slot_1.name: sign
slot_1.desc: Returns -1 if x is negative, 0 if x is zero, and 1 if x is positive.

slot_1.name: ceil
slot_1.desc: The smallest integer greater than or equal to x.

slot_1.name: floor
slot_1.desc: The largest integer less than or equal to x.

slot_2.name: pow
slot_2.desc: Returns the power of the first input raised to the second input.

slot_2.name: mod
slot_2.desc: Returns the remainder of the division of the first input by the second input.

slot_2.name: and
slot_2.python: lambda a, b: a and b
slot_2.desc: Returns True if both inputs are True, otherwise returns False.

slot_2.name: or
slot_2.python: lambda a, b: a or b
slot_2.desc: Returns True if at least one input is True, otherwise returns False.

slot_2.name: xor
slot_2.math: f(a, b) = (a AND not b) OR (not a AND b)
slot_2.python: lambda a, b: (a and not b) or (not a and b)
slot_2.desc: Returns True if exactly one input is True, otherwise returns False.

slot_2.name: absdiff
slot_2.desc: Returns the absolute difference between the two inputs.

slot_2.name: gcd
slot_2.math: f(a, b) = greatest common divisor of a and b
slot_2.desc: Returns the greatest common divisor of the two inputs.

slot_2.name: lcm
slot_2.math: f(a, b) = least common multiple of a and b
slot_2.python: lambda a, b: abs(a*b) // math.gcd(a, b)
slot_2.desc: Returns the least common multiple of the two inputs.

realtime.name: AVGR
realtime.desc: Computes the average of the last `window` number of sensor readings.

realtime.name: COUNT
realtime.desc: Increments a counter whenever an event is true.

realtime.name: DEBOUNCE
realtime.math: `output = signal if (t - last) > delay else last`
realtime.desc: Prevents rapid changes in signal, only updates when signal remains the same for a `delay` duration.

realtime.name: STABILIZE
realtime.math: `output = value if abs(value - last) < threshold else last`
realtime.desc: Filters out noisy data that deviates beyond a `threshold` from the last stable value.

realtime.name: TRIGGER
realtime.math: `output = 1 if condition and not reset else 0`
realtime.desc: Outputs 1 when a condition is met and is not reset; otherwise, outputs 0.

realtime.name: THRESHOLD
realtime.math: `output = 1 if input > high else (0 if input < low else 0.5)`
realtime.desc: Provides a binary or ternary output based on whether input crosses high or low thresholds.

permutation.name: supp
permutation.math: The support of a permutation σ is the set of elements {i | σ(i) ≠ i}
permutation.python: lambda sigma: set(i for i in range(len(sigma)) if sigma[i] != i)
permutation.desc: Returns the support of a permutation, which is the set of elements that are moved by the permutation.

permutation.name: inv
permutation.math: The inverse of a permutation σ is a permutation σ⁻¹ such that σσ⁻¹ = σ⁻¹σ = id
permutation.python: lambda sigma: [sigma.index(i) for i in range(len(sigma))]
permutation.desc: Returns the inverse of a permutation, which is the permutation that reverses the effect of the original permutation.

permutation.name: comm
permutation.math: The commutator of two permutations σ and τ is [σ, τ] = στσ⁻¹τ⁻¹
permutation.python: lambda sigma, tau: lambda s: tau(sigma(tau(s)))
permutation.desc: Returns the commutator of two permutations, which is the permutation that measures how much the two permutations fail to commute.

permutation.name: orb
permutation.math: The orbit of an element i under a permutation σ is {σ^k(i) | k ≥ 0}
permutation.python: lambda sigma: lambda i: [sigma**k(i) for k in range(1 + sigma.index(i))]
permutation.desc: Returns the orbit of an element under a permutation, which is the set of elements that can be reached from the given element by repeatedly applying the permutation.

permutation.name: stab
permutation.math: The stabilizer of an element i under a permutation σ is {τ ∈ S_n | τ(i) = i}
permutation.python: lambda sigma: [tau for tau in itertools.permutations(range(len(sigma))) if tau[sigma.index(i)] == i]
permutation.desc: Returns the stabilizer of an element under a permutation, which is the set of permutations that fix the given element.

permutation.name: conj
permutation.math: The conjugate of a permutation σ by a permutation τ is τστ⁻¹
permutation.python: lambda sigma, tau: lambda s: tau(sigma(tau.index(s)))
permutation.desc: Returns the conjugate of a permutation by another permutation, which is the permutation obtained by applying τ before and after σ.

physical.name: fof
physical.desc: Calculates the force acting on an object when mass and acceleration are known according to Newton's Second Law.

physical.name: pse
physical.desc: Computes the kinetic energy of an object with given mass and velocity.

physical.name: gpe
physical.python: lambda m, g, h: m * g * h
physical.desc: Indicates the gravitational potential energy of an object with mass m, at height h above a reference level, with gravitational acceleration g.

physical.name: tpt
physical.desc: Determines the time taken for an object to travel a certain distance at a constant velocity.

physical.name: wpr
physical.python: lambda F, d, theta: F * d * (lambda t: math.cos(math.radians(t)))(theta)
physical.desc: Calculates the work done by a force when it displaces an object over a distance d at an angle θ with respect to the force direction.

physical.name: nde
physical.desc: Represents the conservation of mechanical energy principle, stating that the change in kinetic energy is equal to the negative change in potential energy.

attention.name: Twist
attention.desc: Permutes the input tensor along two specific dimensions.

attention.name: Slice
attention.desc: Extracts a specified slice from a multi-dimensional tensor.

attention.name: Mix
attention.python: lambda A, B: torch.cat((A, B), dim=1)
attention.desc: Concatenates two tensors along a specified axis.

attention.name: Bump
attention.desc: Swaps two dimensions of a tensor.

attention.name: Scale
attention.desc: Multiplies each element of the tensor by a scalar value.

attention.name: Swap
attention.desc: Reverses the order of elements in specified dimensions.

attention.name: Cube
attention.desc: Reshapes a tensor to have three dimensions of equal size, forming a cube-like structure.

attention.name: Fold
attention.desc: Reduces the tensor's dimensionality by folding all elements into a single dimension.

geometry.name: flip
geometry.desc: A tile that reflects all points across a horizontal line, effectively flipping the cube along this axis.

geometry.name: twist
geometry.desc: A tile that rotates the cube by 90 degrees around a vertical axis, changing the positions of layers orthogonally.

geometry.name: slice
geometry.desc: A tile that scales points along one axis while keeping the other unchanged, effectively slicing the cube into layers.

geometry.name: swap
geometry.desc: A tile that swaps the x and y coordinates, effectively rotating the cube's planes.

geometry.name: skew
geometry.desc: A tile that applies a shear transformation to the cube, creating a slanted effect along one axis.

geometry.name: zoom
geometry.desc: A tile that uniformly scales the cube by a factor, enlarging or reducing its size symmetrically.

geometry.name: warp
geometry.desc: A tile that applies a non-linear transformation to the cube, warping its surface in a unique way.

geometry.name: shift
geometry.desc: A tile that translates the cube by a fixed amount in both axes, sliding it across a plane.

certainty.name: NORM
certainty.math: The normal distribution \( N(\mu, \sigma^2) \), where \( \mu \) is the mean and \( \sigma \) is the standard deviation.
certainty.desc: Represents the probability distribution that describes many natural phenomena and is used extensively in statistics and data modeling.

certainty.name: POIS
certainty.math: The Poisson distribution \( P(\lambda) \), where \( \lambda \) is the average rate of events.
certainty.desc: Used to model the number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space, particularly rare events.

certainty.name: RAND
certainty.math: Random number generation from a uniform distribution \( U(a, b) \).
certainty.desc: Represents the generation of random numbers often used in simulations, games, and as a basis for other distributions.

certainty.name: BINO
certainty.math: The binomial distribution \( B(n, p) \), where \( n \) is the total number of trials and \( p \) is the probability of success in each trial.
certainty.desc: Models the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials each with the same probability of success.

certainty.name: CONF
certainty.math: Confidence intervals provide a range of values which is likely to contain an unknown population parameter with a certain level of confidence.
certainty.desc: Used to estimate the reliability of a sample mean as an estimate of a population mean, providing a range with a known level of confidence.

certainty.name: HYPO
certainty.math: Hypothesis testing with a p-value \( p \) to determine if the null hypothesis can be rejected.
certainty.desc: A statistical method used to evaluate the evidence against a null hypothesis in favor of an alternative hypothesis by calculating a p-value.

certainty.name: REGR
certainty.math: Linear regression \( y = \beta_0 + \beta_1x \), where \( \beta_0 \) is the intercept and \( \beta_1 \) is the slope.
certainty.desc: A method for modeling the relationship between a scalar response and one or more explanatory variables.

certainty.name: RISK
certainty.math: Value at risk (VaR) \( VaR(\alpha, \sigma) \), where \( \alpha \) is the confidence level and \( \sigma \) is the standard deviation.
certainty.desc: A measure of the risk of loss in the value of a risky asset or portfolio over a specific time period for a given confidence interval.

trajectory.name: rot3
trajectory.desc: A 3D rotation matrix based on Rodrigues' rotation formula.

trajectory.name: omg_avg
trajectory.desc: Calculates the average angular velocity over a time period.

trajectory.name: distort
trajectory.desc: Applies a quadratic distortion to the input signal.

trajectory.name: normvec
trajectory.desc: Normalizes a vector to have unit length.

trajectory.name: twist
trajectory.desc: Computes the twist (wedge product) of a velocity vector and an angular velocity vector.

trajectory.name: so3_exp
trajectory.desc: Computes the exponential map of a vector in the Lie algebra so(3) to a rotation matrix in SO(3).

trajectory.name: interp_spline
trajectory.desc: Interpolates a set of points using a cubic spline.

intention.name: TWIST
intention.math: A rotation applied to the Rubik's cube state.
intention.desc: Represents a single rotation on the Rubik's cube, which is a fundamental operation.

intention.name: PERM
intention.math: A permutation of cube layers or slices.
intention.desc: Represents a permutation of layers or slices on the Rubik's cube, which affects the order of pieces.

intention.name: SLICE
intention.math: A cut through the cube that separates a layer or slice.
intention.desc: Represents the act of slicing a Rubik's cube into layers or slices, which can be used to reposition pieces.

intention.name: SOLVE
intention.math: A sequence of rotations and permutations that lead to a solved cube state.
intention.desc: Represents the goal of finding a sequence of operations that results in a solved Rubik's cube.

intention.name: RESET
intention.math: A transformation to reset the cube state to its initial configuration.
intention.desc: Represents the intention to reset the cube to its initial unsolved state.

intention.name: TEST
intention.math: A trial rotation or permutation to evaluate the effect on the cube.
intention.desc: Represents the act of testing a move or combination of moves to assess its effect on the cube's state without permanently altering it.

sensor.name: twst
sensor.desc: A tile that calculates the twist in sensor readings by adding a phase shift to the previous sensor value.

sensor.name: mixm
sensor.desc: A tile that implements an exponential moving average filter for sensor data, useful to smooth out short-term fluctuations and highlight longer-term trends.

sensor.name: roll
sensor.desc: A tile that computes the maximum value from the current sensor reading and the previous n-1 sensor readings, useful for identifying peak values.

sensor.name: sma
sensor.math: y(t) = ∑[ (x(t-k) / n ) for k in range(0, n) ]
sensor.desc: A tile to calculate the simple moving average of a sensor's readings over n time periods, which provides a clearer picture of the overall trend.

sensor.name: sinr
sensor.desc: A tile that calculates a trigonometric sine ratio of the sensor reading with respect to the previous reading, which can be useful in identifying the direction of movement in cyclic patterns.

sensor.name: fir
sensor.desc: A tile that performs a finite impulse response (FIR) filter on sensor data using a set of coefficients to emphasize or de-emphasize certain frequencies.

statistical.name: MIN
statistical.math: \( \text{min}(X) \) is the smallest value in the dataset X.
statistical.desc: Returns the smallest value in a given dataset.

statistical.name: MAX
statistical.math: \( \text{max}(X) \) is the largest value in the dataset X.
statistical.desc: Returns the largest value in a given dataset.

statistical.name: RANGE
statistical.desc: Calculates the difference between the maximum and minimum values in a dataset.

statistical.name: QUART
statistical.math: \( Q_1 \) and \( Q_3 \) are the first and third quartiles respectively.
statistical.desc: Determines the first and third quartiles, representing the 25th and 75th percentiles of the dataset.

statistical.name: IQR
statistical.desc: Calculates the interquartile range, which is the difference between the third and first quartiles.

statistical.name: MODE
statistical.math: \( \text{mode}(X) \) is the value that appears most frequently in the dataset X.
statistical.desc: Finds the most frequently occurring value in a dataset.

statistical.name: MAD
statistical.math: \( \text{MAD}(X) \) is the average absolute deviation from the median.
statistical.desc: Measures the average distance between each data point and the median.

statistical.name: MSE
statistical.math: \( \text{MSE}(X, Y) \) is the mean of the squared differences between corresponding data points in datasets X and Y.
statistical.desc: Calculates the mean of the squared differences between the predicted values and the actual values.

clifford.name: [sph]
clifford.math: S(a) = a * I where I is the metric
clifford.desc: This tile represents the spherical product, applying the metric I to an element, effectively scaling it by its norm.

clifford.name: [exp]
clifford.desc: This tile calculates the exponential of a multivector by summing the series, useful for iterative approximations.

clifford.name: [inv]
clifford.math: inv(a) = a^* / ||a||^2 where a^* is the reverse of a
clifford.desc: This tile calculates the multiplicative inverse of a multivector, taking the reverse and dividing by the squared norm.

clifford.name: [rot]
clifford.python: `def rot(a, b, theta): return (np.cos(theta) + np.sin(theta)*a) * b * (np.cos(theta) - np.sin(theta)*a)`
clifford.desc: This tile performs a rotation of a vector b by angle θ using a bivector a, which represents a plane of rotation.

clifford.name: [refl]
clifford.desc: This tile represents a reflection of a vector b across a plane defined by a, where a is a unit normal vector to the plane.

clifford.name: [wap]
clifford.python: `def wap(a, b): return a * b.rev() + b * a.rev()`
clifford.desc: This tile calculates the anticommutative product of two multivectors a and b, also known as the wedge product, which is essential in representing oriented areas or volumes.
